original_abstract:
The growing popularity of location-based systems, allowing unknown/untrusted
servers to easily collect huge amounts of information regarding users'
location, has recently started raising serious privacy concerns. In this paper
we study geo-indistinguishability, a formal notion of privacy for
location-based systems that protects the user's exact location, while allowing
approximate information - typically needed to obtain a certain desired service
- to be released. Our privacy definition formalizes the intuitive notion of
protecting the user's location within a radius r with a level of privacy that
depends on r, and corresponds to a generalized version of the well-known
concept of differential privacy. Furthermore, we present a perturbation
technique for achieving geo-indistinguishability by adding controlled random
noise to the user's location. We demonstrate the applicability of our technique
on a LBS application. Finally, we compare our mechanism with other ones in the
literature. It turns our that our mechanism offers the best privacy guarantees,
for the same utility, among all those which do not depend on the prior.
humanized_abstract: The recent boom in location-based systems and the ease with which an unknown/untrusted server can gather a huge amount of personal location data from users has raised a number of privacy concerns. In this work, we study the geo-indistinguishability privacy definition for location-based systems. The geo-indistinguishability notion provides a framework that protects a user’s true location while allowing an approximation of the location to be published. The latter is necessary to get the desired service. The geo-indistinguishability definition provides protection for the user location up to some radius r, with a privacy level that depends on r. This definition is a generalized version of the differential privacy definition. We present a perturbation mechanism for achieving the geo-indistinguishability by adding appropriate noise to the user location. We show a case study for an LBS application that illustrates the usability of our privacy mechanism. We also compare our mechanism with other known mechanisms in the literature and show that our mechanism achieves the highest level of privacy, for the same level of utility, in comparison with mechanisms that are not based on prior distribution.

This definition is a generalized version of the differential privacy definition. We present a perturbation mechanism for achieving the geo-indistinguishability by adding appropriate noise to the user location. We show a case study for an LBS application that illustrates the usability of our privacy mechanism. We also compare our mechanism with other known mechanisms in the literature and show that our mechanism achieves the highest level of privacy, for the same level of utility, in comparison with mechanisms that are not based on prior distribution.
